---
title: "Ch 4a — Determinants"
subtitle: "One number from a square matrix — and what it tells you"
lesson-id: "cbse12.determinants"
tier: "cbse12"
ncert-class: 12
ncert-chapter: 4
ncert-chapter-title: "Determinants"
board-exam: true
prerequisites: ["cbse12.matrix-operations"]
unlocks: ["cbse12.matrix-inverse-cramers"]
applied-domains: ["ai-ml", "research"]
estimated-minutes: 50
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [Ch 3b — Matrix Operations](../03b-matrix-operations/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable computing $2\times2$ and $3\times3$ matrix products?
3. Do you know the area formula for a parallelogram?
:::

## Touch

Two vectors $\vec{u} = (3, 1)$ and $\vec{v} = (1, 2)$ start from the
origin. Together they span a parallelogram.

::: {.lesson-beat}

The parallelogram has corners $O = (0,0), \vec{u} = (3,1), \vec{u}+\vec{v}
= (4,3), \vec{v} = (1,2)$. Sketch it on graph paper if you can.

:::

You can compute its area in two ways:

**Way 1 (geometry):** signed area $= u_1 v_2 - u_2 v_1 = 3\cdot2 -
1\cdot1 = 5$.

**Way 2 (formula):** Form the matrix $\begin{pmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 1 & 2
\end{pmatrix}$ — vectors as columns. Its **determinant** is $3\cdot2 -
1\cdot1 = 5$.

Same number. The determinant of a $2\times2$ matrix is the **signed
area** of the parallelogram its columns span (positive if the columns
are oriented counter-clockwise, negative otherwise).

For a $3\times3$ matrix, the determinant is the **signed volume** of
the parallelepiped its columns span. For an $n\times n$ matrix, signed
$n$-dimensional volume.

That picture is the lesson. The formula is just a careful way to
compute the number.

## Wonder

1. What if $\vec u$ and $\vec v$ are parallel — what's the area? What
   does the determinant become?
2. Swap the two columns. Does the area change sign?
3. Multiply one column by 2. Does the area double?
4. Add a multiple of one column to the other. What happens to the area?

## Discover

### Problem 1

Compute $\begin{vmatrix} 4 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{vmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
For $\begin{pmatrix} a & b \\ c & d \end{pmatrix}$, determinant is $ad
- bc$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$4\cdot3 - 2\cdot1 = 10$.
</details>

### Problem 2

Compute $\begin{vmatrix} 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 6 \end{vmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Look at the rows. Are they related?
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$2\cdot6 - 4\cdot3 = 0$. **Row 2 is $1.5\times$ row 1** — they're
proportional. Determinant is zero exactly when rows (or columns) are
linearly dependent.
</details>

### Problem 3

Compute $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \\ 7 & 8 & 10
\end{vmatrix}$ by expansion along the first row.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\det = a_{11} M_{11} - a_{12} M_{12} + a_{13} M_{13}$, where $M_{ij}$
is the $2\times2$ determinant after deleting row $i$, column $j$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$M_{11} = \begin{vmatrix} 5 & 6 \\ 8 & 10 \end{vmatrix} = 50 - 48 = 2$.
$M_{12} = \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 6 \\ 7 & 10 \end{vmatrix} = 40 - 42 = -2$.
$M_{13} = \begin{vmatrix} 4 & 5 \\ 7 & 8 \end{vmatrix} = 32 - 35 = -3$.
$\det = 1\cdot2 - 2\cdot(-2) + 3\cdot(-3) = 2 + 4 - 9 = -3$.
</details>

### Problem 4

Compute $\begin{vmatrix} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 7 & 3 & 0 \\ 9 & 8 & 2
\end{vmatrix}$ — a lower-triangular matrix.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Expand along the *first row* — many zeros.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\det = 5 \cdot \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 0 \\ 8 & 2 \end{vmatrix} - 0 + 0 =
5 \cdot 6 = 30$. **Determinant of a triangular matrix is the product of
the diagonal.**
</details>

### Problem 5

Without computing all three, decide which is zero and why:
(a) $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 4 & 5 & 6 \end{vmatrix}$
(b) $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 7 & 8 & 9 \end{vmatrix}$
(c) $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(a) zero** — a row of zeros. **(b) zero** — two equal rows.
**(c)** $= 1$ — diagonal product. Identity matrix has $\det = 1$.
</details>

You've now expanded along rows, used triangular structure, and seen
that a zero row or a repeated row kills the determinant.

::: {.fun-fact}
The schoolbook trick for $3\times3$ determinants — write the matrix,
copy the first two columns to the right, and add the three "diagonals
down" while subtracting the three "diagonals up" — is **Sarrus's rule**,
published by **Pierre Frédéric Sarrus** in 1833. Sarrus was a quiet
provincial professor in Strasbourg, and his rule worked only for $3
\times 3$ matrices, but it gave generations of students a memorable
mnemonic for a calculation that otherwise looks like book-keeping.
The deeper formula — cofactor expansion — was already known to **Vandermonde**
(1771) and **Laplace** (1772), and is what NCERT teaches because it
generalises to every size. Sarrus's rule is the picture; cofactor
expansion is the proof.
:::

::: {.key-insight}
A $2 \times 2$ determinant is the **signed area** of a parallelogram;
a $3 \times 3$ determinant is the **signed volume** of a parallelepiped.
Every property in *Name* — sign flip on row swap, scaling by $k$ when a
row is scaled, vanishing on equal rows — is a *geometric* fact in
disguise. Two parallel rows give a flat (degenerate) parallelogram with
zero area; that is *exactly* what "linearly dependent rows" means
algebraically. The determinant is geometry written as arithmetic.
:::

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

For a square matrix $A = [a_{ij}]_{n \times n}$, the **determinant**
$|A| = \det(A)$ is defined recursively. For $n = 1$, $|[a]| = a$. For
larger $n$, the **expansion along row $i$** is
$$\det(A) = \sum_{j=1}^{n} (-1)^{i+j} a_{ij} M_{ij},$$
where $M_{ij}$ is the **minor** — the determinant of the
$(n-1)\times(n-1)$ matrix obtained by deleting row $i$, column $j$.
The signed minor $C_{ij} = (-1)^{i+j} M_{ij}$ is the **cofactor**.

**Key properties** (square $A, B$):

- $|A^T| = |A|$.
- Swap two rows (or columns): $|A|$ flips sign.
- Multiply one row by $k$: $|A|$ multiplies by $k$.
- Two equal rows (or columns): $|A| = 0$.
- Add a multiple of one row to another: $|A|$ unchanged.
- $|AB| = |A|\cdot|B|$.
- $|kA| = k^n |A|$ for $n\times n$ matrices.

A matrix is **singular** if $|A| = 0$, **non-singular** otherwise.

:::

A verse:

> *Cofactors signed in checkerboard pattern;*
> *Expand by any row, the answer's the same.*
> *Equal rows? Zero. Triangular? Diagonal product.*
> *Singular = no inverse — that's the determinant's name.*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Find $|A|$ for $A = \begin{pmatrix} 1 & -1 & 2 \\ 3 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 4 &
> 5 \end{pmatrix}$, expanding along column 2.

A competent solver thinks:

1. Column 2 entries: $-1, 0, 4$. The $0$ kills one term.
2. Cofactor signs: $-, +, -$ (column 2 of the checkerboard).
3. $|A| = (-1)\cdot(-1)\cdot M_{12} + 0 + 4\cdot(-1)\cdot M_{32}$.
4. $M_{12} = \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{vmatrix} = 13$.
   $M_{32} = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 1 \end{vmatrix} = -5$.
5. $|A| = 1 \cdot 13 + (-4)\cdot(-5) = 13 + 20 = 33$.

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** $\begin{vmatrix} 7 & 2 \\ 5 & 3 \end{vmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$21 - 10 = 11$.
</details>

**W2.** $\begin{vmatrix} \cos\theta & -\sin\theta \\ \sin\theta &
\cos\theta \end{vmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$. (Rotation matrix has determinant 1
— it preserves area.)
</details>

**W3.** Expand along the first column:
$\begin{vmatrix} 2 & 3 & 1 \\ 0 & 4 & -1 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{vmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Triangular: $2\cdot4\cdot5 = 40$.
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Find $x$ if $\begin{vmatrix} x & 2 \\ 18 & x \end{vmatrix} =
\begin{vmatrix} 6 & 2 \\ 18 & 6 \end{vmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Compute both determinants.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
LHS $= x^2 - 36$. RHS $= 36 - 36 = 0$. So $x^2 = 36 \Rightarrow x = \pm
6$.
</details>

**P2.** Show that $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c &
c^2 \end{vmatrix} = (b-a)(c-a)(c-b)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Apply $R_2 \to R_2 - R_1$ and $R_3 \to R_3 - R_1$. Then expand.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\begin{vmatrix} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 0 & b-a & b^2-a^2 \\ 0 & c-a & c^2-a^2
\end{vmatrix}$. Factor $b-a, c-a$ from rows 2, 3:
$(b-a)(c-a)\begin{vmatrix} 1 & a & a^2 \\ 0 & 1 & b+a \\ 0 & 1 & c+a
\end{vmatrix}$. Expand: $(b-a)(c-a)\cdot1\cdot((c+a)-(b+a)) =
(b-a)(c-a)(c-b)$. ✓ This is the **Vandermonde determinant**.
</details>

**P3.** Find $|3A|$ if $|A| = 4$ and $A$ is $3\times3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$|kA| = k^n|A|$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|3A| = 3^3 \cdot 4 = 108$.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** Without expansion, prove $\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ a & b & c
\\ a^3 & b^3 & c^3 \end{vmatrix} = (a-b)(b-c)(c-a)(a+b+c)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Use column operations $C_2 \to C_2 - C_1$ and $C_3 \to C_3 - C_1$;
factor; expand.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
After $C_2 \to C_2 - C_1, C_3 \to C_3 - C_1$:
$\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ a & b-a & c-a \\ a^3 & b^3-a^3 & c^3-a^3
\end{vmatrix}$. Expand along row 1:
$= (b-a)(c^3 - a^3) - (c-a)(b^3 - a^3)$
$= (b-a)(c-a)(c^2+ca+a^2) - (c-a)(b-a)(b^2+ba+a^2)$
$= (b-a)(c-a)(c-b)(c+b+a)$
$= (a-b)(b-c)(c-a)(a+b+c)$ after sign rearrangement. ✓
</details>

### Board-Pattern Practice

**1-mark MCQ #1.** $\begin{vmatrix} \cos15° & \sin15° \\ \sin75° &
\cos75° \end{vmatrix} = $ (a) 0 (b) 1 (c) $\cos60°$ (d) $\sin60°$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$\sin75° = \cos15°$, $\cos75° = \sin15°$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$\cos15° \cos75° - \sin15° \sin75° = \cos15°\sin15° - \sin15°\cos15° =
0$. **(a) 0.**
</details>

**1-mark MCQ #2.** If $|A| = 5$ and $A$ is $3\times3$, then $|2A^T| =$
(a) 10 (b) 40 (c) 80 (d) 25.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$|2A^T| = 2^3 |A^T| = 8\cdot5 = 40$. **(b).**
</details>

**Assertion–Reason.** *Assertion (A):* If two rows of a $3\times3$
matrix are identical, $|A| = 0$. *Reason (R):* The determinant changes
sign under row swap; equal rows force $|A| = -|A|$.

(a) Both A and R true; R correct explanation. (b) Both true; R not
correct explanation. (c) A true, R false. (d) A false.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Both true; R is exactly the reason. **(a).**
</details>

**2-mark.** Find values of $x$ for which $\begin{vmatrix} 3 & x \\ x & 1
\end{vmatrix} = \begin{vmatrix} 3 & 2 \\ 4 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$3 - x^2 = 3 - 8 = -5 \Rightarrow x^2 = 8 \Rightarrow x = \pm 2\sqrt{2}$.
</details>

**3-mark.** Show that $\begin{vmatrix} a-b-c & 2a & 2a \\ 2b & b-c-a &
2b \\ 2c & 2c & c-a-b \end{vmatrix} = (a+b+c)^3$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$R_1 \to R_1 + R_2 + R_3$. Each entry of the new $R_1$ becomes $a+b+c$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
After $R_1 \to R_1 + R_2 + R_3$: each new $R_1$ entry equals $a + b +
c$. Factor out $a+b+c$:
$(a+b+c) \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2b & b-c-a & 2b \\ 2c & 2c &
c-a-b \end{vmatrix}$.
Now $C_2 \to C_2 - C_1$ and $C_3 \to C_3 - C_1$:
$(a+b+c)\begin{vmatrix} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 2b & -b-c-a & 0 \\ 2c & 0 &
-a-b-c \end{vmatrix}$.
Triangular: $= (a+b+c) \cdot 1 \cdot (-(a+b+c)) \cdot (-(a+b+c)) =
(a+b+c)^3$. ✓
</details>

**3-mark.** Find the area of the triangle with vertices $(2, 7), (1, 1),
(10, 8)$ using determinants.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
Area $= \frac{1}{2}|D|$ where $D = \begin{vmatrix} x_1 & y_1 & 1 \\ x_2
& y_2 & 1 \\ x_3 & y_3 & 1 \end{vmatrix}$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$D = \begin{vmatrix} 2 & 7 & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 10 & 8 & 1 \end{vmatrix}
= 2(1-8) - 7(1-10) + 1(8-10) = -14 + 63 - 2 = 47$.
Area $= 47/2 = 23.5$ sq units.
</details>

**5-mark long answer.** Using properties of determinants, show
$\begin{vmatrix} x+a & b & c \\ a & x+b & c \\ a & b & x+c \end{vmatrix}
= x^2(x + a + b + c)$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$C_1 \to C_1 + C_2 + C_3$. Each entry of $C_1$ becomes $x + a + b + c$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$C_1 \to C_1 + C_2 + C_3$:
$(x+a+b+c)\begin{vmatrix} 1 & b & c \\ 1 & x+b & c \\ 1 & b & x+c
\end{vmatrix}$.
$R_2 \to R_2 - R_1, R_3 \to R_3 - R_1$:
$(x+a+b+c)\begin{vmatrix} 1 & b & c \\ 0 & x & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & x
\end{vmatrix}$.
Triangular: $(x+a+b+c)\cdot 1\cdot x \cdot x = x^2(x+a+b+c)$. ✓
</details>

**6-mark Case Study.** *Land area from GPS coordinates.*

A surveyor records three corners of a triangular plot at coordinates
$A = (1, 5), B = (3, -1), C = (6, 4)$ (in some unit-grid).

(i) Use the determinant formula to compute the plot's area. (3 marks)
(ii) The next year, all three points have shifted by $(+2, +3)$ due to
re-surveying. Show that the area is unchanged. (1 mark)
(iii) If the same shape is scaled up by a factor of 4 (every coordinate
multiplied by 4), what is the new area, and how does the determinant
formula confirm it? (2 marks)

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**(i)** $D = \begin{vmatrix} 1 & 5 & 1 \\ 3 & -1 & 1 \\ 6 & 4 & 1
\end{vmatrix} = 1(-1-4) - 5(3-6) + 1(12+6) = -5 + 15 + 18 = 28$. Area
$= 14$.
**(ii)** Adding a constant to all three pairs of coordinates corresponds
to translating; you can verify the determinant remains 28 by computing.
Geometrically, translation preserves area.
**(iii)** Scaling each coordinate by 4 multiplies each row's first two
entries by 4. The determinant scales by $4^2 = 16$ (because it's a 2D
area; one factor of 4 from $x$, one from $y$). New area $= 14\cdot16 =
224$.
</details>

::: {.callout-note}
**NCERT Exemplar pointer:** Ex 4.1, 4.2 — properties Q4–10.
**Previous-year pointer:** CBSE 2023 Set 3 Q26 (similar to 5-mark);
CBSE 2022 Q2 (similar to MCQ #2).
:::

### Takeaways

If you remember nothing else from this lesson, remember these:

1. **The determinant of a square [matrix](/glossary.qmd#matrix) is one number that captures whether its rows (or columns) are linearly independent** — non-zero means they span full dimension; zero means they collapse onto each other.
2. **Cofactor expansion lets you expand along any row or column**; pick the one with the most zeros to do the least work.
3. **Row operations are the determinant's friends**: adding a multiple of one row to another leaves $|A|$ unchanged, swapping rows flips its sign, and scaling a row by $k$ multiplies $|A|$ by $k$ — these three rules turn brutal $n \times n$ calculations into clean triangular ones.

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"$|A + B| = |A| + |B|$."** False, almost always. Determinant is
   not linear in the matrix as a whole — only in each *row* separately.
2. **"$|kA| = k|A|$."** Only when $A$ is $1 \times 1$. For $n \times n$,
   $|kA| = k^n|A|$.
3. **"Row swap = same determinant."** No — row swap *flips the sign*. A
   common error in long determinant calculations.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you compute a $3\times3$ determinant by row reduction in five
   minutes?
2. Did you solve at least 5 board-pattern items unaided?
3. Can you state when a matrix is singular and what that means
   geometrically?

- **3 yes:** move on.
- **2 yes:** redo the 5-mark long-answer problem.
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover* with paper.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- [`cbse12.matrix-operations`](../03b-matrix-operations/index.qmd) —
  matrix arithmetic.

### What this unlocks

- [`cbse12.matrix-inverse-cramers`](../04b-matrix-inverse-cramers/index.qmd)
  — inverse uses the determinant.

### NCERT chapter alignment

- **Class 12, Chapter 4 — Determinants** (NCERT 2023 rationalised).
  Sections 4.1–4.4.
- **Sister synthesis lesson:** [`t5.matrices`](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/index.qmd).
- Read alongside the NCERT textbook.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI/ML.** The determinant of a covariance matrix appears in the
multivariate normal density. Log-determinants are used in variational
inference and normalising flows. A near-zero determinant warns of
collinear features.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Research / engineering.** Jacobian determinants govern the change-of-
variables formula in multiple integrals. Stability analyses of physical
systems hinge on the sign of a determinant.
:::

---

::: {.callout-note collapse="true"}
## Author's notes

- **Touchstones:** 3Blue1Brown ("the determinant is the area scaling
  factor"), Bhāskara (verse), Gelfand (problem-first).
:::
